story:
Adults are strange. They do things like “garden,” ignore their phones when they ring, and think a perfect weekend involves salad, fish, early bedtimes, and coffee at a hotel with no pool. Meanwhile, I have great ideas—world-changing ones. I’m talking about the Food Transformer and a Back‑to‑Life Potion. I just have to figure out how to build them between school, sports, and chores. Last week I built a tiny leaf bridge for an ant colony, rescued a caterpillar from the lawn mower, and fought off giant squirrels with a foam rocket launcher that wouldn’t aim right—in my backyard saga slash video game. So, yeah, time is tight. Also, don’t tell anyone—someone already “invented” my flying car idea.

The Food Transformer comes in two versions. Version One needs a very responsible doctor to make it safe and hygienic. Picture dinner: chicken and broccoli. Chicken is fine; broccoli? Hard pass. So there’s a countertop tube, about the size of a toilet paper roll, that grinds the broccoli into microscopic bits. A thin, sterile hose connects the unit to a tiny, doctor‑installed port near your belly button. The machine filters out anything you’re allergic to and sends only the nutrients straight to your stomach. No taste buds involved. Problem solved. Bruh.

Then there’s Food Transformer II, even better because there’s no belly‑button port. You feed the broccoli into the tube and it comes out tasting like strawberry or mango while still looking like broccoli and still being healthy. It’s kind of like that Willy Wonka thing, only real, and mine, because nobody’s actually built it yet. I’ll get there first.

I have more ideas. Make video games come to life. Invent teleportation so I never have to ride to school listening to Mom’s music or get honked at because Dad forgets his turn signal. I wish fish could talk. I wish I didn’t need sleep so I wouldn’t miss anything.

My biggest idea is a Back‑to‑Life Potion. It’s more of a wish, really—the good kind of witches’ brew you see in Halloween movies, not evil. The formula would be super specific and different for every creature, and nothing would harm animals or people. Think symbolic ingredients or plant‑based stand‑ins, the sciencey kind that represent what each being loves.

Take dogs. They definitely get a potion—sorry, scorpions, you probably don’t need one. The dog mix would include safe, pretend versions of dirt, sticks, mud, and bark—plus grass, tennis‑ball fuzz, and “bones,” as in the peanut‑butter‑flavored chew kind. For cats: catnip, found feathers, and toy mouse heads, obviously. You see the logic.

The main one is for humans, because when someone you love is gone, you want them back. That formula would start with a gentle base and then be customized, because people have different diets and allergies. Maybe the base is grape juice, flower petals, a little sugar, and cinnamon, with movie ingredients like eye of newt and frog parts swapped for safe, pretend versions such as gummy candies and herbal blends. Some would need orange juice; others, cranberry. It would be about healing, not hurting—bringing comfort and memory to life again.

Now I just have to interview some doctors, fix my schedule, and keep my notes hidden so no one steals my ideas.
